dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a sick person at Phillip Morris.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, I've been feeling short of breath and lightheaded for about 30 minutes now.
Mr. Smith, what is your name?
It's Eric.
What county are we in?
[CITY] County, I believe.
What is the date today?
July 30th.
What happened today?
I was talking to my boss and suddenly I felt lightheaded and couldn't catch my breath.
Have you had any chest pain or other symptoms?
No, it's mostly the shortness of breath.
Do you have any known chronic medical conditions?
I have high blood pressure and asthma.
Mr. Smith, had you been taking your medications?
I ran out a few days ago and haven't replaced them yet.
We are going to take your pulse now, sir.
Let me check how many times your chest rises and falls in one minute.
Right now, your respiratory rate is 22 per minute.
Mr. Smith, we're now going to measure your blood pressure.
Are you wheezing when you breathe?
Yes, I've been wheezing a lot.
We will now check your blood sugar levels.
Any difficulty or pain when you swallow?
No, just breathing is hard.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow score of 15.
Can I listen to your lungs with a stethoscope?
That's fine.
I'm hearing wheezing in both lungs during inspiration and expiration.
I'll now check your oxygen level with this clip on your finger.
Your pulse rate is 85 per minute, and your capillary refill is less than 2 seconds.
Your blood pressure is 183 over 126.
Your blood sugar is 64 mg/dL.
I'll apply these patches and monitor your heart rhythm continuously. We're also doing a 4-lead ECG now for continuous rhythm monitoring per protocol.
Any drainage or trouble swallowing?
No, I'm not having those issues.
I'll now check your skin for rashes or abnormal color.
I don't see anything unusual.
Does moving your limbs cause any pain or soreness?
No, I can move freely and feel my limbs without issue.
The monitor shows a sinus rhythm with arrhythmia and no immediate signs of cardiac arrest.
Your airway is patent, breath sounds are diminished with wheezing, and peripheral pulses are regular and strong.
We're going to give you a nebulizer treatment to help with your airway obstruction.
You'll receive 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Atrovent via a nebulizer with your nasal cannula at 15 LPM.
I'll insert an IV in your left arm to prepare for any needed intravenous fluids or medications.
I have placed 200 cc of normal saline in your IV for hydration and preparedness, using an 18G catheter with aseptic technique per protocol.
Your breathing rate is now 16 times a minute.
Your heart rate is down to 73 and your blood pressure is 201 over 129.
Since the nebulizer, your breathing has definitely improved, and you appear to be less in distress now.
Pulse oximetry is at 94%, and your oxygen saturation has increased to 99% since the intervention started.
Your vital signs remain stable, and your respiratory status has improved, though not fully resolved yet.
We'll continue to monitor you and do another 12-lead EKG shortly as part of our protocol for suspected cardiac conditions.
The latest EKG shows signs of an inferoapical infarct and a left anterior fascicular block, so we're going to document that now.
Your latest blood pressure is 201 over 143, heart rate 70, and oxygen saturation is 98% as we continue monitoring with 4-lead rhythm strips.
You're breathing more comfortably and still receiving 15 LPM of oxygen via nasal cannula, which we'll keep constant throughout transport.
Your vitals now show 191 over 119 blood pressure, heart rate 83, respiration rate 17, and SpO2 at 97%. Things are trending in a stable direction right now.
Our latest check shows your respiratory rate is now 19 breaths per minute, indicating some maintenance of improvement.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how bad is your pain, sir?
I don't have any pain right now.
We will assume semi-fowlers position during transport for comfort and optimal breathing to help with your respiratory distress.
Wheels up, Mr. Smith. Let us know if you need help remaining comfortable during the ride to [COMPANY_NAME].
I feel a little better, thank you.
This is a 51-year-old male with a history of hypertension and moderate asthma. No known drug allergies except for penicillin, per the chart review.
He did not report any pain and was administered 324 mg of aspirin before we arrived, which is consistent with local protocol guidelines for suspected myocardial infarction.

Interventions include a nebulized Duoneb, 18G IV with 200 cc normal saline, and non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM with current vitals at 193/122, 77 pulse, 97 SpO2, and continuous 4-lead ECG monitoring per protocol 3-11 for medical respiratory distress/asthma.
The patient is conscious and oriented and has been transported to [COMPANY_NAME] Hospital Emergency Department for further evaluation of his asthma exacerbation and potential cardiac findings.
